title: Light, Stitched Together
description:
These images were created as a result of a class assignment for PHOT-031: Intermediate Photography at Solano Community College where we had to create a conceptual project where “the medium is the message”.

Though each of the following images may appear to be normal, they are not. I created this series of images with only one, single candle. Therefore, an image was captured for each candle that you see in each of the provided final images. I left my camera in the same place, took a series of images with the candle being in different locations, and then in post, I composited the images together using Photoshop.

Some of my inspiration comes from Hiroshi Sugimoto, in that his images of the theaters don’t immediately appear to be any different from a quick snapshot of a theater with the screen overexposed. It’s not until after his process is revealed, is it where the meaning comes to fruition.

For Sugimoto's theater images, the meaning of the work emerges not from what is depicted, but from how the images were created. Through long-exposure photography, Sugimoto transforms a full-length film into a single, static image of a glowing screen that records the passage of time. The film is effectively erased and compressed into this white glow, which illuminates the theater and replaces the moving image with a trace of accumulated light.

For my candle images, the meaning too doesn’t emerge from what is being depicted, but instead from how the images were created. Through digital composition, I merge a series of images together to create a scene that never truly existed in the real world. Though the light illuminates its surroundings and correctly displays what existed in that space, the space itself cannot be fully seen without the composition of the series of images. Each instance of light represents a different moment in time, yet they are presented simultaneously, collapsing multiple moments into a single frame. In this way, the image constructs an artificial sense of presence, where one candle appears as many, and time is reorganized into a unified, but impossible, instant. The meaning, thus, lies in the medium’s ability to manipulate time, space, and light, showing that photography can exist not just as a simple record of reality, but as a process capable of constructing it.

Date(s) of Creation: 04/07/2026 - 04/11/2026
Camera(s): Fujifilm XE-1
Lens(es): 7artisans 18mm f/6.3
Editing Software(s): Adobe Lightroom Classic and Adobe Photoshop
Location(s) Shot: Home

title: Vintage Eyes
description:
These images were created as a result of a class assignment for PHOT-031: Intermediate Photography at Solano Community College where we had to create a project that showed “self as subject”.

I own several vintage film cameras. The images you see before you were captured with a digital camera through the viewfinder of one of those vintage film cameras. I tried to pick cameras from my collection that had different style viewfinders to give each image a different visual aesthetic. Some cameras used were the Minolta X-700, Polaroid SX-70, Mamiya C33, Pilot Six, Rolleiflex Automat K4A, and Nikon F.

I completed this project in my room during nightly hours, so many of these shots required long exposures and/or flash.

Date(s) of Creation: 03/07/2026
Camera(s): Fujifilm XE-1
Lens(es): Minolta MD W.Rokkor-X 28mm f/2.8
Editing Software(s): Adobe Lightroom Classic
Location(s) Shot: Home

title: Blossoms & Bokeh
description:
These were some of the first images I made with my Soviet-Made Helios 44-2 lens. This lens is known for its pleasant swirly bokeh, and I decided to put it to the test during almond blossom season.

Date(s) of Creation: 02/14/2026
Camera(s): Fujifilm XE-1
Lens(es): Helios 44-2 58mm f/2
Editing Software(s): Adobe Lightroom Classic
 Location(s): HWY 26 near Linden, CA

title: Just Another Photo Walk
description:
These images were created as a result of a class assignment for PHOT-031: Intermediate Photography at Solano Community College where we had to create a collection of “still life“ images.

My subject of choice was this little photographer lego, and I thought it would be a neat idea if I photographed the lego posing for the camera and posing as if it was taking photos. It turned out to be a fun and interesting process, as it was fun to move the lego around my backyard trying to find the perfect scene. What I really like about these images, is that for me, they seem to bring the little lego photographer to life. And because it is macro photography, the photos make the small, surrounding object feel as if they are bigger than they actually are, creating a sort of magical feeling. To me, these images represent the wonder of childhood.

Date(s) of Creation: 02/06/2026 - 02/08/2026
Camera(s): Fujifilm XE-1
Lens(es): Vivitar 55mm f/2.8 Auto Macro
Editing Software(s): Adobe Lightroom Classic
Location(s) Shot: Home
